What makes the wind blow?: The sun heats some parts of the ground more than others. The warm air over those parts rises up toward the sky. Cooler air near the ground rushes in to take its place. This rushing air is really the wind that we feel. So wind is just air moving from cool spots to warm ones.
Why do icebergs float?: A giant block of ice in the sea is called an iceberg. When water freezes, it spreads out and fills a bit more space. This leaves the ice a bit less heavy than the sea water. Because it weighs less, the ice floats up near the top. Most of the ice still hides below the water line.
How do fish see underwater?: Fish have eyes that are made to work under water. Light from above sinks down and spreads through the water. The light bounces off things and goes into their eyes. Their eyes gather the light and send a picture to the brain. This is how a fish can see well while it swims.
Why do we cry?: Tears come from tiny parts, called glands, just above your eyes. Big feelings send word to those glands to make more tears. The tears well up and spill over, running down your face. Crying can rinse your eyes and help ease those big feelings. So tears come out when big feelings build up in you.
What is inside an egg?: Inside an egg there is a yellow part and a clear part. The yellow part, called the yolk, is food for a baby bird. The clear part around it works like a soft cushion. A hard shell wraps around it all to keep it safe. Together these parts help a baby bird grow inside.
Why do stars appear in different pattern: Stars sit in many spots spread far away in space. From the earth they look like tiny bright dots at night. Our eyes join those scattered dots into shapes and lines. Long ago people saw shapes in them and gave them names. So the patterns come from how our eyes link the dots.
Why do people get sunburned?: The sun gives off strong rays that our eyes cannot see. Too much of this ray can burn the skin on your body. Your skin turns red and feels hot, sore, and tender. That red, sore skin is really what we call a sunburn. So we wear hats and shade to guard against strong sun.
How does a seed know when to grow?: A seed rests in the ground and waits for the right time. It feels the warmth around it and drinks up some water. Warmth and water are the signals that spring has come. These signs wake the seed and tell it to start growing. Then a tiny root grows down and a small green stem pushes up.
What causes lightning?: Inside a storm cloud, bits of ice bump and rub together. This rubbing builds up static, the same zap you feel from a doorknob. The charge grows bigger until it jumps as a huge spark. That giant spark leaps through the sky as lightning. So lightning is a big spark made high up in the clouds.
What do insects do in the winter?: Winter grows far too cold for most bugs to fly around. Many of them hide under loose bark or in the ground. Their little bodies slow right down so they can rest. They stay tucked away through the cold, dark winter days. When warm days come back, the resting bugs wake up again.
How does a car move?: A car needs fuel to go, and fuel is a kind of gas. The car burns the fuel inside a part called the engine. The burning makes a strong push that turns other parts. Those parts spin the wheels around and around fast. The turning wheels grip the road and roll the car along.
How do squirrels find food in the winter: In the fall, squirrels hide nuts down in the ground. They bury the nuts in many small hiding spots. Their sharp nose helps them smell the nuts under the dirt. They dig the nuts back up to eat in the cold winter. So a good nose helps them find the food they hid.
How do we smell things?: Things around you give off tiny bits into the air. You breathe those floating bits high up into your nose. Special spots inside your nose catch and read the bits. They send a quick word to your brain about the smell. Your brain then tells you just what you are smelling.
What is pressure?: A push on something is what we mean by pressure. The harder you press, the more pressure you make. Air and water push on things all the time. The deeper you dive, the more the water pushes on you. So pressure is just how hard something is being pushed.
Why do we have to sleep?: Your body works hard all through the long busy day. Sleep gives your body a chance to rest and fix itself. While you sleep, your brain sorts out all that you learned. In the morning you wake up fresh and full of go. So sleep is the way your body and mind get set again.
How does the sun make sunlight?: The sun is a giant ball of very hot gas. Deep inside it, tiny bits crash and join up. This crashing makes a huge burst of heat and light. That light streams out and travels across space. Some of it reaches us as the sunlight we see.
What is photosynthesis?: Green plants can make their own food using the sunlight. Their leaves soak up the light and mix it with air and water. This mixing makes a sweet food that feeds the growing plant. The plant uses that food to build roots, stems, and leaves. So sunlight is what lets a green plant feed itself.
What makes ice slippery?: A thin layer of water always rests on top of the ice. Your shoe slides on this wet layer with almost no grip. There is little for the bottom of your shoe to hold. So your feet slide away and you lose your footing. That wet layer on top is what makes the ice so slick.
Why do we get hungry?: Your body burns food all day to give you power to move. After a while, the food you ate gets used up inside. Your empty belly sends a signal up to your brain. The brain reads it and makes you feel that hungry pang. Eating fills you back up and stops the hungry feeling.
What makes popcorn pop?: A corn seed has a drop of water shut in a hard case. When the stove heats it, that water turns into steam. The steam pushes hard on the shell from the inside. The shell can only take so much before it bursts. The little seed flips inside out with a loud little pop.
Why do we have bones?: Bones make up the hard frame that holds your body up. Without them your body would flop like a soft rag doll. Your bones give your muscles a firm place to pull against. They also guard soft parts, like the ribs around your heart. Strong bones let you stand, walk, run, and jump.
How does soap clean our hands?: Dirt and grease cling to your skin and water alone slides past them. Soap is built to grab grease on one end and water on the other. Its grabby ends grab onto the grease stuck to your hands. When you rinse, the water pulls the soap and grease away. Your hands come out clean because the soap dragged the mess off.
How do birds build nests?: A bird needs a safe, snug spot to keep its eggs warm. It hunts for twigs, grass, mud, and soft bits of fluff. The bird weaves the twigs into a bowl with its beak and feet. It lines the inside with the soft stuff to cushion the eggs. The finished nest holds the eggs safe until they hatch.
Why does a ball bounce?: A rubber ball is springy, so it can squish and spring back. When it drops on the floor, the bottom squashes flat a bit. The squashed rubber pushes hard to snap back to its round shape. That push shoves the ball up off the ground again. The ball bounces less each time until it comes to a stop.
Why does the ocean have waves?: Most waves in the sea start from wind that blows across it. The moving air drags on the top and lifts it into bumps. Each bump rolls along as a wave that heads to the shore. The water mostly bobs in place as the wave moves through it. When a wave meets the shallow sand, it tips over and breaks.
How do plants drink water?: A plant sips water from the soil using its thin roots. The water creeps into tiny tubes that run up the stem. It climbs the tubes all the way up to the leaves. The plant uses that water to make food and stay firm. Sun on the leaves pulls more water up, like a straw.
Why do we yawn?: A yawn is a big, slow breath you take with a wide mouth. You often yawn when you feel tired or worn out. The deep breath may pull in extra air to wake you up. It may also help cool your busy brain down a bit. That is why a good yawn can leave you feeling fresher.
How does rust form on metal?: Rust is the crumbly orange coat that forms on old iron. It grows when iron is left out in both water and air. Bit by bit, the iron joins with the air to turn into rust. Water speeds this up, so wet metal rusts much faster. A coat of paint keeps the air and water off the metal.
What makes a sound loud or soft?: A sound starts when a thing shakes and stirs the air into waves. A big, strong shake pushes tall waves through the air. Those tall waves hit your ears and you hear a loud sound. A small, gentle shake makes short little waves instead. Short waves are weaker, so the sound comes through soft.
Why does the moon shine at night?: The moon cannot make any light of its own at all. It shines because the sun lights up one side of it. That bright side bounces sunlight all the way to us. We see that bounced light shining back as the glow of the moon. When clouds hide the moon, the bounced light cannot reach us.
